title: Computer modeling for discretionary rest predictions
abstract: Techniques for improving computer modeling for generating predictions are described herein. In one example, a computer model is generated to examine data representing time slots in a schedule. In particular, the computer model can be configured to calculate a homeostatic pressure at a first slot in the schedule and determine (1 ) whether the calculated value of homeostatic pressure at the first slot exceeds a preset threshold and (2) whether the first slot corresponds to a preset status. In response to positive determinations to both, the computer model can iteratively search for a second slot in a portion of the schedule that precedes the first slot such that discretionary operations at the second slot would reduce the homeostatic pressure at the first slot to not exceed the threshold. Thus, the computer model can predict the discretionary operations even without relying on historical records of such operations.
claims:
Claims CLAIMS I/We claim: 1. A process of computer modeling for discretionary rest predictions, comprising: executing, with a computer processor, instructions to initiate a loop to examine data representing a plurality of sequential slots of time in a schedule, wherein to examine the plurality of sequential time slots includes: calculating a value of homeostatic pressure at a slot in the schedule; determining (1 ) whether the calculated value of homeostatic pressure at the slot exceeds a preset threshold and (2) whether the slot has a preset status associated with being on duty; and in response to determining that the calculated value of homeostatic pressure at the slot exceeds the preset threshold and that the slot has the preset status associated with being on duty, iteratively searching for one or more slots in a portion of the schedule that precedes the slot such that discretionary rest at the one or more slots reduces the homeostatic pressure at the slot to not exceed the preset threshold; and outputting data representing a prediction that discretionary rest would occur at the one or more slots in the schedule without relying on any historical records of such discretionary rest in the schedule. 2. The process of claim 1 wherein calculating the value of homeostatic pressure at the subsequent slot in the schedule includes: determining whether a data field in an array corresponding to a preceding slot contains data indicating sleep during the preceding slot; in response to determining that the data field in the array contains data indicating sleep during the preceding slot, decrementing a value of homeostatic pressure at the preceding slot by a preset amount; 

 in response to determining that the data field in the array contains data indicating being awake during the slot under examination, incrementing the value of homeostatic pressure at the preceding slot by another preset amount; and setting the value of homeostatic pressure at the slot to be the decremented or incremented value of homeostatic pressure. 3. The process of claim 1 wherein the discretionary rest at the one or more slots reduces the homeostatic pressure at the slot to a new value not exceeding the preset threshold and is less than 10%, 5%, or 1 % from the preset threshold. 4. The process of claim 1 wherein the portion of the schedule that precedes the subsequent slot has a preset duration of twenty four, thirty six, or forty eight hours. 5. The process of claim 1 wherein iteratively searching for the one or more slots includes: defining the portion of the schedule that precedes the slot as a lookback window; and identifying a subset of slots in the defined lookback window, the subset of slots individually: having a level of homeostatic pressure not exceeding the preset threshold; not having the preset status; and having not been examined for discretionary rest. 6. The process of claim 1 wherein iteratively searching for the one or more slots includes: defining the portion of the schedule that precedes the slot as a lookback window; identifying a subset of slots in the defined lookback window, the subset of slots individually: having a level of homeostatic pressure not exceeding the preset threshold; not having the preset status; and 

 having not been examined for discretionary rest; determining whether the identified subset of slots include at least one slot; and in response to determining that the identified subset of slots includes not one slot, indicating additional discretionary rest is not possible. 7. The process of claim 1 wherein iteratively searching for the one or more slots includes: defining the portion of the schedule that precedes the slot as a lookback window; identifying a subset of slots in the defined lookback window, the subset of slots individually having a level of homeostatic pressure not exceeding the preset threshold, not having the preset status, and having not been examined for discretionary rest; and within the identified subset of slots, selecting a slot corresponding to a level of homeostatic pressure higher than all others in the identified subset of slots; determining whether discretionary rest at the selected slot would reduce homeostatic pressure at the slot under examination to not exceed the preset threshold; and in response to determining that discretionary rest at the selected slot would reduce homeostatic pressure at the subsequent slot to not exceed the preset threshold, generating data representing a prediction that discretionary rest would occur at the selected slot in the identified subset of slots. 8. The process of claim 1 wherein iteratively searching for the one or more slots includes: defining the portion of the schedule that precedes the slot as a lookback window; identifying a subset of slots in the defined lookback window, the subset of slots individually having a level of homeostatic pressure not exceeding the preset threshold, not having the preset status, and having not been examined for discretionary rest; and within the identified subset of slots, 

 selecting a slot corresponding to a level of homeostatic pressure higher than all others in the identified subset of slots; determining whether discretionary rest at the selected slot would reduce homeostatic pressure at the slot under examination to not exceed the preset threshold; and in response to determining that discretionary rest at the selected slot would not reduce homeostatic pressure at the slot under examination to not exceed the preset threshold, marking the selected slot as having been examined; and identifying a new subset of slots in the defined lookback window, the new subset of slots individually having a level of homeostatic pressure not exceeding the preset threshold, not having the preset status, and having not been examined for discretionary rest; and repeating the foregoing selecting and determining operations within the identified new subset of slots. 9. The process of claim 1 wherein iteratively searching for the one or more slots includes: defining the portion of the schedule that precedes the slot as a lookback window; identifying a subset of slots in the defined lookback window, the subset of slots individually having a level of homeostatic pressure not exceeding the preset threshold, not having the preset status, and having not been examined for discretionary rest; and within the identified subset of slots, selecting a slot corresponding to a level of homeostatic pressure higher than all others in the identified subset of slots; determining whether discretionary rest at the selected slot would reduce homeostatic pressure at other slots in the identified subset of slots to be below another preset threshold; and in response to determining that discretionary rest at the selected slot would reduce homeostatic pressure at the other slots in the identified subset of slots to be below the another preset threshold, 

 generating data representing a prediction that discretionary rest would not occur at the selected slot in the subset of slots. 10. A computing device, comprising: a processor; and a memory operatively coupled to the processor, the memory containing instructions executable by the processor to cause the computing device to perform a process according to one of claims 1 -9.
